info.title: BoldTrail BackOffice API Documentation
info.description:
## BASICS BoldTrail BackOffice provides an API that can be used to create, retrieve, update, and delete virtually all of the resources stored and manipulated by the system. This interface incorporates several elements of the **REST** tradition, making access to these actions consistent and intuitive. In a nutshell: collections are accessed via a URL ending in a plural noun such as `.../transactions`, and to target an individual resource instance, append its numerical id `.../transactions/85`. To tell the API whether you are viewing, updating, or deleting the resource, make use of the “HTTP verbs,” `GET`, >`PUT`, `PATCH` and `DELETE`. To create a new resource, send a `POST` request to the collection’s URL `.../transactions`. The API’s response will be the content of the requested resource(s) or the data from the as-updated resource. The BoldTrail BackOffice API allows client software to retrieve and operate on a user’s data by making HTTP requests to transfer **JSON-encapsulated** data. Because the BoldTrail BackOffice API is accessible by anything that can make HTTP requests, not only can API client software be written in almost any available programming language, but people can interact with it directly through any tool that’s capable of making requests and receiving responses. ## AUTHORIZATION All API requests have to be authenticated with credentials for a specific BoldTrail BackOffice account, on whose behalf the request is being made. Credentials are currently a single per-account user **API Key** and has the same access level as the user whom the key belongs to.

The API Key has to be passed with each API request as `api_key` parameter: ```http GET /api/v1/users?api_key=..... ``` ## REQUESTS BoldTrail BackOffice API uses **JSON** encoding. When sending `POST` or `PUT` requests, request body should be encoded to **JSON** format. We also require sender to include a `Content-Type: application/json` header indicating type of the request body. Request example: ```http POST /api/v1/users?api_key=..... Content-Type: application/json { "first_name": "John", "last_name": "Doe", "email": "test@example.com", "role": "Office administrator" } ```

tags.name: API LIMITS
tags.description:
### BoldTrail BackOffice API limits
To ensure the best user experience and platform performance, requests through the BoldTrail BackOffice API are rate-limited. Current limits:
- 100 requests per 10 seconds limit
- 2000 requests per hour limit
- 100 failed requests per hour

### Best practices for avoiding rate limiting

If you make multiple API requests in a short period of time, you will reach the API request limit. After that,
BoldTrail BackOffice will stop processing all requests until the next cycle starts. BoldTrail BackOffice account rate limits are
listed in the API limits section at brokermint.com/api. Consider looking at them before starting an integration project.

Below we prepared a set of pro-tips for using our API without exceeding the limits

### Track your API activity

You can interpret the following response headers to confirm the account’s current
rate limit and monitor the number of requests remaining in the current **Burst** (10 seconds) period:

```
  X-Brokermint-Burst-Limit: 100
  X-Brokermint-Burst-Remaining: 99
```

and **Hour** period:

```
  X-Brokermint-Hour-Limit: 2000
  X-Brokermint-Hour-Remaining: 1999
```

and **Failed** requests numbers:

```
  X-Brokermint-Errors-Limit: 100
  X-Brokermint-Errors-Remaining: 90
```

### Catch errors caused by limit exceeding

For each request, you can check if the rate limit has been reached.
If you’ll see the `429 "Too many requests"` status code, it means that you have reached the rate limit.

We recommend **adding code to your script that catches `429` responses**.
If the script ignores the `"Too Many Requests"` error and continues to try to make requests,
subsequent requests will be blocked until the beginning of the next cycle.

For example, a rate limited request may return the following response headers:

```
  < HTTP/1.1 429
  < Server: nginx/1.4.2
  < Date: Wed, 15 Jun 2022 20:16:47 GMT
  < Connection: keep-alive
  < Status: 429
  < X-Brokermint-Burst-Limit: 100
  < X-Brokermint-Burst-Remaining: 56
  < X-Brokermint-Hour-Limit: 2000
  < X-Brokermint-Hour-Remaining: 0
  < X-Brokermint-Errors-Remaining: 0
  < X-Brokermint-Errors-Limit: 100
  < X-Brokermint-Hour-Reset: 1655326800000
  < Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=864000
  < Content-Length: 155
```

Your JSON response body will look like:

```
  {"status": 429, "error": "You have fired too many requests. Please follow API limitation policies. For more information, please visit our API documentation."}
```

The response headers contains the following key information:

+ `Status: 429`
+ `X-Brokermint-Hour-Remaining: 0`
+ `X-Brokermint-Hour-Reset: 1655326800000`

The `429` status code means you’ve exceeded the rate limit. The `X-Brokermint-Hour-Remaining` header
shows that the requestor reached hour limit, and `X-Brokermint-Hour-Reset` specifies
a **13-digit Unix timestamp** that indicates when the next cycle starts, and you’ll be allowed to make API calls.
Your code should stop making additional API requests until enough time has passed to retry.

The following example shows an easy way to detect rate limiting errors:

```
  response = request.get(url)
  headers = response.headers

  if headers["Status"] equals 429:
    if headers["X-Brokermint-Hour-Remaining"] equals 0:
      alert("Hour rate limited. Waiting to retry ...")
      wait_until(headers["X-Brokermint-Hour-Reset"])
      retry(url)

    if headers["X-Brokermint-Burst-Remaining"] equals 0:
      alert("Burst rate limited. Waiting to retry ...")
      wait_until(Time.next_10_seconds_begins)
      retry(url)
```

> The code above is a pseudo-code example that is intended to help you build your own algorithm for catching 429 errors.

### Reduce the number of requests

Make sure you make only the requests that you need. Here’s the list of things to consider when assessing
your integration architecture and API requests:

1. Delete requests if they are not providing information further used within your platform.

2. Cache frequently used data on the server or on the client side using DOM storage. You can also save
relatively static information in a database or serialize it in a file.

3. Instead of making a batch of requests, use webhook subscriptions to listen for events and
ensure your data is up to date. This will allow your integration to
automatically perform actions when a webhook event is received.

4. Use batch endpoints (such as “List available transactions”, which allows
you to get up to 1000 transactions within a single request),
instead of making 1000 different API requests for each single transaction.

### Consider using throttling process

If you regularly approach or bump into the rate limit, consider including a process in your
code that regulates the rate of your requests so that they’re distributed more evenly over time.
This is known as a **throttling process** or a **throttling controller**. Regulating the request rate
can be done statically or dynamically. For example, you can monitor your request rate and
regulate requests when the rate approaches the rate limit.

> To determine if you need to implement a throttling process, monitor your request errors.
How often do you get 429 errors? Does the frequency warrant implementing a throttling process?

### Frequently Asked Questions

**Q**: *Is there an endpoint that returns all endpoints for a resource, including rate limits and times to retry?*

**A**: No, we don’t provide a rate-limit endpoint.

However, we do provide the following response headers that you can use to confirm the account’s
current rate limit and monitor the number of requests remaining in the current minute:

```
  X-Brokermint-Burst-Limit: 100
  X-Brokermint-Burst-Remaining: 50
  X-Brokermint-Hour-Limit: 2000
  X-Brokermint-Hour-Remaining: 1950
  X-Brokermint-Errors-Limit: 100
  X-Brokermint-Errors-Remaining: 90
  X-Brokermint-Hour-Reset: 1655343500000
```

**Q**: *What happens when the rate limit is reached?*

**A**: The request is not being processed; you’ll get a 429 response code.
If you reached Burst limit (100 requests per 10 seconds), you are able to
wait until new 10 seconds period will start to continue requests.

Also, if you are reached Hour limit (2000 requests per hour), you’ll need
to wait until new hour begins. The X-Brokermint-Hour-Reset header
(13-digit Unix timestamp) will help you to determinate, when next cycle starts.

However, if you got more than 100 errored responses during the hour, your integration will be required to wait until next cycle starts.

**Q**: *Will batching calls reduce the number of API calls? How about making parallel calls?*

**A**: No, batching calls won’t reduce the number of API calls. However, using a batch endpoint will reduce calls.

tags.name: UPLOAD DOCUMENTS
tags.description:
### How file uploading works​

In BoldTrail BackOffice, we allow users to upload files through our API using the simple logic: request the upload link and then upload the file.

> Due to our constant commitment to customer security, we allow uploading files like PDF, JPG, DOCX and so on. [See the full list of allowed formats here.](https://support.brokermint.com/en/articles/7125998-security-measures-file-upload-at-brokermint)

Depending on the module, the request's URI and set of parameters will vary, but the overall logic will remain the same and include 2 simple steps:

**STEP 1: Get the upload document link**

Use the **"Request upload URL"** endpoint to generate the new upload document link. Keep in mind that the link will expire in 5 minutes since the creation.

Request example:

```
POST /api/v1/..../request_upload_url
```

Response example:
```json
{
  "upload_url": "http://example.com/upload_document?data=JhsDcf_wey%a27gasu..."
}
```


**STEP 2: Upload the document**

Use this link along with the ``"file"`` [Multipart](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc1341/7_2_Multipart.html) parameter to upload the document to the system.  As a result, you will receive a `200 OK` response with the uploaded document information as a JSON.

Request example:
```
POST http://example.com/upload_document?data=JhsDcf_wey%a27gasu...

Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary="boundary"

--boundary
Content-Disposition: form-data; name="file"; filename="sample.pdf"
Content-Type: application/pdf

<file body>
```

Response example:
```json
{
  "id": 82,
  "name": "sample.pdf",
  "task_id": null,
  "content_type": "application/pdf",
  "has_package": false,
  "review_status": null,
  "upload_status": "complete",
  "status": "ready",
  "url": "http://link.to.file/sample.pdf"
}
```

tags.name: TRANSACTION COMMISSIONS
tags.description:
To add commission items or replace all commission items for an existing transaction use [transaction commission items endpoint](#tag/TRANSACTION-COMMISSIONS/paths/~1v1~1transactions~1{transaction_id}~1commissions/post). To create a transaction with commissions, use the [create transaction endpoint](#tag/TRANSACTIONS/paths/~1v1~1transactions/post) with the `commission_items` attribute included into the request body, along with transaction attributes (see [Sample request](#transactions-commissions-sample-request)). This attribute must be a list of commission item objects (see [Commission item structure](#commission-item-structure)). A minimal set of `commission_items` must contain `tgc` (Total gross commission), `award_distribution`, `award_allocation` and `sales_volume` items. All the commission item's attributes are mandatory, except for the `tgc` item, where `side` must be absent or set to `null`. A transaction can, for example, have only one `award_distribution` commission item per side (eg.) and one `award_allocation` / `sales_volume` / `split` commission item per side and payee. Every `item_type` has its own list of available units of measure (uom), you can find the complete list of available items types and their units of measure below. ### Commission item structure ``` { "item_type": string, // (see "List of available item types and unit of measures" below for a full list of available item types) "side": string, // ("buying" or "listing"), "payer_id": integer, "payer_type:" string, "payee_id": integer, "payee_type": string, "tiers": [ { "low_limit": float, "value": float, "uom": string // (see "List of available item types and unit of measures" below for a full list of available unit of measures) } ] } ``` The `split` commission item can be configured using the following additional attributes: ``` { "sliding_base": string, // ("gross commission", "company dollar contribution", "sales volume", "transaction points", "agent commission", "commission basis" or "split: company's portion") "sliding_base_period": string, // ("year to date", "month to date", "month of the acceptance date", "since last anniversary", "last 12 months to the current date", "last 12 months to the end of the previous month", "custom range" or "this transaction only") "sliding_base_accrued_by": string // ("agent", "team") } ``` ### List of available item types and units of measures | Item Type | Unit of Measure | | -------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | `tgc` | `% of price sold`, `$` | | `award_distribution` | `% of price sold`, `% of tgc`, `$` | | `award_allocation` | `% of shared gross income`, `$` | | `sales_volume` | `% of price sold`, `$` | | `split` | `% of remaining balance`, `% of tgc`,
`% of side income`, `% of commission basis`, `$` | ### Create Transaction With Commissions Sample request ``` POST https://my.brokermint.com/api/v2/transactions?api_key={{YOUR_API_KEY}} Content-Type: application/json { "address": "1150 W 14th Street", "city": "Upland", "state": "CA", "zip": "91786", "status": "listing", "price": 1000000.0, "transaction_type": "traditional sale", "representing": "seller", "acceptance_date": null, "expiration_date": null, "closing_date": null, "listing_date": null, "buying_side_representer": { "id": 4212407, "type": "Contact" }, "listing_side_representer": { "id": 6014, "type": "Account" }, "commission_items": [ { "item_type": "tgc", "side": null, "payer_id": 6014, "payer_type": "Account", "payee_id": 6014, "payee_type": "Account", "tiers": [ { "low_limit": 0.0, "value": 6.0, "uom": "% of price sold" } ] }, { "item_type": "award_distribution", "side": "listing", "payer_id": 6014, "payer_type": "Account", "payee_id": 6014, "payee_type": "Account", "tiers": [ { "low_limit": 0.0, "value": 3.0, "uom": "% of price sold" } ] }, { "item_type": "award_distribution", "side": "buying", "payer_id": 4212407, "payer_type": "Contact", "payee_id": 4212407, "payee_type": "Contact", "tiers": [ { "low_limit": 0.0, "value": 3.0, "uom": "% of price sold" } ] }, { "item_type": "award_allocation", "side": "listing", "payer_id": 6014, "payer_type": "Account", "payee_id": 36736, "payee_type": "User", "tiers": [ { "low_limit": 0.0, "value": 80.0, "uom": "% of shared gross income" } ] }, { "item_type": "sales_volume", "side": "listing", "payer_id": 36736, "payer_type": "User", "payee_id": 36736, "payee_type": "User", "tiers": [ { "low_limit": 0.0, "value": 80.0, "uom": "% of price sold" } ] }, { "item_type": "split", "side": "listing", "payer_id": 6014, "payer_type": "Account", "payee_id": 36736, "payee_type": "User", "sliding_base": "sales volume", "sliding_base_period": "year to date", "sliding_base_amount": 0.0, "sliding_base_accrued_by": "agent", "tiers": [ { "low_limit": 0.0, "value": 80.0, "uom": "% of remaining balance" }, { "low_limit": 100000.0, "value": 90.0, "uom": "% of remaining balance" } ] }, { "item_type": "award_allocation", "side": "listing", "payer_id": 6014, "payer_type": "Account", "payee_id": 36467, "payee_type": "User", "tiers": [ { "low_limit": 0.0, "value": 20.0, "uom": "% of shared gross income" } ] }, { "item_type": "sales_volume", "side": "listing", "payer_id": 36467, "payer_type": "User", "payee_id": 36467, "payee_type": "User", "tiers": [ { "low_limit": 0.0, "value": 20.0, "uom": "% of price sold" } ] }, { "item_type": "split", "side": "listing", "payer_id": 6014, "payer_type": "Account", "payee_id": 36467, "payee_type": "User", "sliding_base": "gross commission", "sliding_base_period": "year to date", "sliding_base_accrued_by": "agent", "tiers": [ { "low_limit": 0.0, "value": 80.0, "uom": "% of remaining balance" } ] } ] } ```

tags.name: SSO
tags.description:
SSO API allows remotely login user into the BoldTrail BackOffice.
To login a user into the BoldTrail BackOffice website using sso token,
redirect the user to the URL: `https://my.brokermint.com/users/sign_in_by_token?token=<token>`

tags.name: WEBHOOKS
tags.description:
Webhook subscriptions allow you to listen for events you are interested in so your integration can automatically perform required actions. ### Building a webhook endpoint Before creating a subscription, you need to create a webhook endpoint on you server which will be POSTed for every event occurrence on BoldTrail BackOffice side in JSON format. To acknowledge an incoming event, your endpoint must return HTTP 200 status code within 10 seconds. Otherwise, the notification attempt is repeated. See [Retry policy](#webhooks-retry-policy) for more information. ### Creating a subscription To create a subscription, use [create API endpoint](#tag/WEBHOOKS/paths/~1v1~1webhooks/post) using your webhook endpoint URL as `callback_url`. Every account's user is limited to 5 subscriptions. After subscription created, it needs to be [Activated](#tag/WEBHOOKS/paths/~1v1~1webhooks~1{id}~1activate/post) to start receiving webhook events. **Make sure to save the `secret_token` from the response, you won't be able to see it again.** ### Security Every event notification includes `X-Brokermint-Webhook-Signature` HTTP header containing the request's signature (HMAC Hex Digest). This allows you to verify that the events were sent by BoldTrail BackOffice, not by a third party. BoldTrail BackOffice generates signatures using a hash-based message authentication code ([HMAC](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hash-based_message_authentication_code)) with [SHA-256](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SHA-2). To verify the signature, you need to compute an HMAC Hex Digest with the SHA256 hash function using your subscription's `secret_token` as the key and the request body as the message and compare the result with the signature in the header. ### Test your webhook endpoint To test your webhook endpoint you can use [test API endpoint](#tag/WEBHOOKS/paths/~1v1~1webhooks~1:id~1test/post). It sends a test event to your webhook endpoint with `{"value": "ping"}` as an `object`. ### Retry policy Every failed event notification attempt is repeated over next 4 hours with the following interval — 5, 10, 15, 30, 60 and 120 minutes. After 6 failures to send the notification, BoldTrail BackOffice marks the event as failed, deactivates the webhook subscription and emails you about the deactivation. ### Event deliverability BoldTrail BackOffice does not guarantee delivery of events in the order in which they are generated and immediately after an entity was created/updated/deleted. It may take up to 2 minutes to deliver the event since it's been triggered. Also, your webhook endpoint might occasionally receive the same event more than once, or the `delete|removed` event could be false positive (sent before the `create` message). Therefore, we advise you to log the events you've processed and not process already logged events, considering the sequence of events. ### Event object The Webhook payload is sent in standard JSON format and contains an event object with the following information represented by key-value pairs: - `event_id` - A unique event ID - `event_type` - The lifecycle event that triggered the payload (e.g. transaction.created; transaction.updated; etc.) - `api_version` - The API version - `object` - The object containing the relevant information of the affected entity - `metadata` - The data which is passed to the webhook subscription when created - `created_at` - 13-digit unix timestamp of the time at which the event trigger occurred ### List of available event types | Event type | Description | Object structure | API version | |-------|-------|-------|-------| | transaction.created | Occurs whenever a new transaction is created | [response sample](#tag/TRANSACTIONS-V2/paths/~1v2~1transactions/post) | 2 | | transaction.updated | Occurs whenever a transaction is updated | [response sample](#tag/TRANSACTIONS-V2/paths/~1v2~1transactions/post) | 2 | | transaction.deleted | Occurs whenever a transaction is deleted | `{ "id": 23,"deleted_at": 1679667148337,"external_id": "" }` | 2 | | transaction.participant.added | Occurs whenever a participant is added to transaction | [user response sample](#tag/TRANSACTION-PARTICIPANTS/paths/~1v1~1transactions~1{transaction_id}~1participants~1users/post);
[contact response sample](#tag/TRANSACTION-PARTICIPANTS/paths/~1v1~1transactions~1{transaction_id}~1participants~1contacts/post) | 1 | | transaction.participant.updated | Occurs whenever a transaction participant is updated | [user response sample](#tag/TRANSACTION-PARTICIPANTS/paths/~1v1~1transactions~1{transaction_id}~1participants~1users/post);
[contact response sample](#tag/TRANSACTION-PARTICIPANTS/paths/~1v1~1transactions~1{transaction_id}~1participants~1contacts/post) | 1 | | transaction.participant.removed | Occurs whenever a participant is removed from transaction | `{ "id": 123456, "bm_transaction_id": 123456, "contact_id": 123456, "type": "contact" }`
or
`{ "id": 123456, "bm_transaction_id": 123456, "user_id": 123456, "type": "user" }` | 1 | | incoming_transaction.accepted | Occurs whenever a incoming transaction accepted | [ShortIncomingTransaction response sample](#tag/INCOMING-TRANSACTIONS/paths/~1v1~1incoming_transactions~1{id}/get) | 1 | | incoming_transaction.discarded | Occurs whenever a incoming transaction discarded | [ShortIncomingTransaction response sample](#tag/INCOMING-TRANSACTIONS/paths/~1v1~1incoming_transactions~1{id}/get) | 1 | | buyer_offer.created | Occurs whenever a new buyer offer is created | [response sample](#tag/BUYER-OFFERS/paths/~1v1~1buyer_offers/post) | 1 | | buyer_offer.updated | Occurs whenever a buyer offer is updated | [response sample](#tag/BUYER-OFFERS/paths/~1v1~1buyer_offers/post) | 1 | | buyer_offer.deleted | Occurs whenever a buyer offer is deleted | `{ "id": 123456 }` | 1 | | buyer_offer.participant.added | Occurs whenever a participant is added to buyer offer | `{ "id": 123456, "participant_type": "contact", "participant_id": 654321, "role": "Agent", "owner": false, "first_name": "Jonh", "last_name": "Doe", "email": "jonh.doe@example.com" }`
or
`{ "id": 123456, "participant_type": "user", "participant_id": 654321, "role": "Agent", "owner": false, "first_name": "Jonh", "last_name": "Doe", "email": "jonh.doe@example.com" }` | 1 | | buyer_offer.participant.updated | Occurs whenever a buyer offer participant is updated | `{ "id": 123456, "participant_type": "contact", "participant_id": 654321, "role": "Agent", "owner": false, "first_name": "Jonh", "last_name": "Doe", "email": "jonh.doe@example.com" }`
or
`{ "id": 123456, "participant_type": "user", "participant_id": 654321, "role": "Agent", "owner": false, "first_name": "Jonh", "last_name": "Doe", "email": "jonh.doe@example.com" }` | 1 | | buyer_offer.participant.removed | Occurs whenever a participant is removed from buyer offer | `{ "id": 123456, "buyer_offer_id": 123456, "participant_id": 654321, "participant_type": "contact" }`
or
`{ "id": 123456, "buyer_offer_id": 123456, "participant_id": 654321, "participant_type": "user" }` | 1 | ### Event example ``` { "event_id": "df6d06fe-cc2b-422d-9737-52b30a48d4f7", "event_type": "transaction.created", "api_version": "2", "object": { "address": "17019 Broken Rock Court", "city": "Riverside", "state": "CA", "zip": "92503", "status": "listing", ... }, "metadata": {}, "created_at": 1604964452345 } ```

API_KEY.description:
All API requests have to be authenticated with credentials for a specific BoldTrail BackOffice account,
on whose behalf the request is being made. Credentials are currently a single per-account user API Key and
has the same access level as the user whom the key belongs to.
The API Key has to be passed with each API request as `api_key` parameter.
API_KEY.name: api_key

countParam.name: count
countParam.description: Specifies the number of items to retrieve

sortParam.name: sort
sortParam.description:
Specifies the order of items to retrieve. From more recent to oldest or vice versa.
Default value is "asc"

startingFromIdParam.name: starting_from_id
startingFromIdParam.description: Specifies the ID of entity to retrieve records starting from

full_info.name: full_info
full_info.description: Specifies whether to retrieve short or full participant information.

external_id.description:
external identifier passed during user creation,
this value available through users API only,
users can be filtered by this field to see whether such user already exists

permissions.description: Permissions, can be a subset of the enabled permissions only

commission_plan_ids.description: List of commission plan ids that you want to apply for newly created user

onboarding_template_id.description: ID of agent onboarding checklist template

onboarding_template_assignees.description: Array of agent onboarding template assignees. Each role for the provided template checklist template must be set.

role.description: Role name from the provided onboarding checklist template

My Custom Field.description:
Below are your user's custom fields
They depends upon Account fields settings

Address.description: custom field that depends on your account setting

City.description: custom field that depends on your account setting

State.description: custom field that depends on your account setting

Zip.description: custom field that depends on your account setting

user_id.description:
the ID of the user, it's the same value as "id" field
this ID will be the same for this user across all accounts this user participates

account_user_id.description:
uniquely identifies user-to-account association
it's unique for this user in each account

custom_id.description:
Customized user ID shown on UI as "User ID"
it's the read-only calculated value, in most of the cases this is the "user_id"
customizations like adding office ID or showing external_id instead can be requested

avatar_added.description: indicates whether user has non-default avatar

user_id.description:
the ID of the user, it's the same value as "id" field
this ID will be the same for this user across all accounts this user participates

account_user_id.description:
uniquely identifies user-to-account association
it's unique for this user in each account

custom_id.description:
Customized user ID shown on UI as "User ID"
it's the read-only calculated value, in most of the cases this is the "user_id"
customizations like adding office ID or showing external_id instead can be requested

external_id.description:
external identifier passed during user creation,
this value available through users API only,
users can be filtered by this field to see whether such user already exists

close_unapproved_transactions.description:
Can close not approved transactions.
Depends on "Can close transactions" permission.

edit_transaction_commissions.description:
Can edit transaction commissions.
Depends on "Can view all transaction commissions" permission.

generate_cda.description:
Can generate CDA.
Depends on "Can view all transaction commissions" permission.

change_award_distribution.description:
Can change award distribution.
Depends on "Can view all transaction commissions" permission.

finalize_commissions.description:
Can finalize commissions.

owner.description:

Indicates whether this person is the owner of transaction

always.description: If set, checklist will always be applied
If not set, "when" conditions will be applied. If "when" condition is not set the transaction will be matched on it by default.

closing_date.description: 13-digit unix timestamp (Expected without hours, minutes, or seconds. If included, these values will be cleared.)

listing_date.description: 13-digit unix timestamp (Expected without hours, minutes, or seconds. If included, these values will be cleared.)

timezone.description: transaction timezone in hours from UTC

403.description: User does not have permission to submit documents

post.description:
Approves a document for review. This endpoint supports both first-level and second-level approvals.

Requires either first-level or second-level document review permissions.

403.description: User does not have permission to approve documents

post.description:
Rejects a document during review. This endpoint supports both first-level and second-level rejections.

Users with either first-level or second-level document approval permissions can use this endpoint.
A rejection comment is required.

403.description: User does not have permission to reject documents

post.description:
This allows reviewers to undo their approval decision and send the document back for revision.

Requires either first-level or second-level document review permissions.

403.description: User does not have permission to revoke approvals

example.notes:
A batch of documents for the transaction 123 Main St have been reviewed.

Here are the results:
APPROVED: 1
REJECTED: 0

The resulting list will be limited by at most 1000 records.

parameters.name: statuses

parameters.name: created_since

parameters.name: updated_since
parameters.description:
Filter transactions updated since specified date.

parameters.name: closed_since
parameters.description:
Filter transactions closed since specified date.
Date format: YYYY-MM-DD or 13-digit unix timestamp

parameters.name: closing_date_range
parameters.description:
Filter transactions that have closing_date within specified date range.
it expects valid Unix timestamp range in a form of: `{from date}-{to date}`
e.g. `closing_date=1556697600-1557165600`
translates to:
from `1556697600` (Wed, 01 May 2019 00:00:00 -0800)
to `1557165600` (Mon, 06 May 2019 10:00:00 -0800)
also you can use shortcuts like: - today - yesterday - tomorrow - in_the_past - in_a_future - week_to_date - current_week - last_week - last_2_weeks - next_week - month_to_date - current_month - last_month - last_2_months - last_3_months - next_month - next_2_months - next_3_months - same_month_last_year - quarter_to_date - current_quarter - last_quarter - q1_of_this_year - q2_of_this_year - q3_of_this_year - q4_of_this_year - same_quarter_last_year - ytd - current_year - last_year - next_year

shortcut.summary: date range that covers last month

Permanently deletes buyer offer and all associated with it data including documents.
In most of the cases updating buyer offer state to "withdrawn" is preferable to deletion.

parameters.name: replace_todo_tasks

This parameter allows you to remove all incomplete to-do tasks from the buyer offer before adding a new checklist.
It set to `false` by default and all the new tasks will be added on top of existing ones.

Filter incoming transactions by specified statuses.
Allowed values: `new`, `accepted`, `discarded`, `unverified`

Filter incoming transactions updated since specified date.
Date format: YYYY-MM-DD or 13-digit unix timestamp

parameters.description:
Filter transactions by source.
This can be the name of CRM or other system that you sourced these transactions,
this allows to distinct incoming transactions with the same external IDs from different sources

parameters.description: Filter incoming transactions by the comma separated list of external IDs

parameters.name: full_info
parameters.description:
Specifies whether to retrieve short or full incoming transaction information.
List is returned in short format by default.

post.description:
Create or update incoming transactions.
Update will happen in case incoming transaction with the same `external_id` and `source_id` already exists.
**Note:** if existing transaction was already *accepted* of *declined*, its updates will be ignored.

source_id.description:
Incoming transaction source
this can be the name of CRM or other system that you sourced these transactions,
this allows to distinct incoming transactions with the same external IDs from different sources

200.description: List of identifiers for created incoming transactions

get.summary: List of filters and available filter options for specified report

get.description:
**Note:** Report data is limited. The maximum number of rows returned is not more than `1000`

All query parameters except `timezone` are filters (see Report Filters above), in a form `field=filter_value`

Below we show reference set of parameters which covers all data types so it'll be clear how to use them.

parameters.name: timezone
parameters.description: timezone you need date fields and filters to be interpreted in

parameters.name: representing
parameters.description:
optional filter by ‘representing’ column
it expects one or more of the available_options received in “filters” response

parameters.name: status
parameters.description:
optional filter by ‘status’ column
it expects one or more of the available_options received in “filters” response

parameters.name: closing_date
parameters.description:
optional filter by ‘closing_date’ column
it expects valid Unix timestamp range in a form of: `{from date}-{to date}`
e.g. `closing_date=1556697600-1557165600`
translates to:
from `1556697600` (Wed, 01 May 2019 00:00:00 -0800)
to `1557165600` (Mon, 06 May 2019 10:00:00 -0800)
also you can use shortcuts like: - today - yesterday - tomorrow - in_the_past - in_a_future - week_to_date - current_week - last_week - last_2_weeks - next_week - month_to_date - current_month - last_month - last_2_months - last_3_months - next_month - next_2_months - next_3_months - same_month_last_year - quarter_to_date - current_quarter - last_quarter - q1_of_this_year - q2_of_this_year - q3_of_this_year - q4_of_this_year - same_quarter_last_year - ytd - current_year - last_year - next_year

shortcut.summary: date range that covers last month

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: Specifies the number of items to skip before retrieving the result list

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: Specifies the number of items to retrieve, max 1000.

200.description: Report data. (**Note:** The actual set of columns will depend on your report configuration)

parameters.name: with_documents
parameters.description:
Set to 1 to retrieve only tasks that require or have documents.
List is returned all transaction tasks by default.

get.summary: List of tasks in specified transaction checklists

parameters.name: with_documents
parameters.description:
Set to 1 to retrieve only tasks that require or have documents.
By default, list will contain all tasks in the specified transaction checklist.

post.summary: Create a new tasks in specified transaction checklists

post.description:
Transitions the task to the 'complete' status. The task must meet all requirements
(document uploaded if required, no pending reviews) before it can be completed.

400.description: Task cannot be completed due to invalid state

post.description:
Transitions the task from 'complete' status back to 'todo' status.
This allows reopening a previously completed task.

400.description: Task cannot be uncompleted due to invalid state

get.summary: List of all uncompleted transaction's tasks
get.description: The resulting list will be limited by at most 200 records.

200.description: List of all uncompleted transaction's tasks

get.description:
The resulting list will be limited by 5 records at most.
Following parameters allow to paginate and filter the output.

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: Specifies the number of items to retrieve

post.description: Note that subscription needs to be activated first to start receiving webhook events.

put.description:
Allows to update an existing webhook subscription.
The update will affect only specified fields. All other fields will preserve their values.

\* *updating callback_url deactivates webhook subscription*

\* *updated_at and created_at are read-only fields*

400.description: Webhook subscription not activated, request to callback_url failed

post.summary: Send asynchronous ping event to webhook subscription's callback url
